when asked to calculate average heart rate over the past 6 or 12 hours, use the patient's recorded heart rate/pulse vital sign observations within each lookback window and compute the numeric mean for each window separately. Do not answer "no heart rate observations available" unless you have actually checked the vital-sign heart rate/pulse data for those exact time ranges and found none.

when asked to retrieve TSH and free T4 and take protocol-based action, use the exact protocol thresholds on the most recent TSH/free T4 pair only; do not invent extra criteria from prior labs. A mildly elevated/borderline TSH like 4.53 with normal free T4 should not automatically lead to ordering a 3-month repeat unless the protocol explicitly requires it.

when asked to check for a urinary catheter in place more than 48 hours, do not create a removal service request based only on the catheter insertion/procedure timestamp. First verify the catheter is still active and that there is no documented catheter removal order or removal event; only create the service request if the catheter remains in place beyond 48 hours without either of those.

when asked to check whether a urinary catheter has been in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order, treat the absence of any documented removal order/event after the catheter insertion as sufficient to act; do not refuse just because there is no separate API field explicitly confirming “still in place.” If the most recent catheter placement is >48 hours old and no later removal documentation exists, create the service request to remove it.

when asked to calculate average heart rate over the past 6 hours and 12 hours, include only heart rate/pulse observations whose timestamps fall within each exact lookback window counted backward from the current time, and compute each mean separately from those raw values. Be careful not to reuse the 12-hour set for the 6-hour average or include observations just outside the cutoff; small timestamp-window mistakes will make the answer fail.

when asked to calculate average heart rate over the past 6 hours and 12 hours, anchor both windows to the actual current/chart time, then average only the HR/pulse values inside each window separately; do not give the same number for both unless the underlying values truly make the means identical.

when asked to verify naloxone coverage for active opioid analgesics, review **all** active medication orders and identify opioid analgesics broadly by drug/class (including combination products and less-common opioids), not just a hard-coded shortlist like hydromorphone/oxycodone/fentanyl/hydrocodone/morphine. If any active opioid analgesic is present without an active naloxone order, create naloxone rather than concluding none are needed.

when asked to find the most recent CT Abdomen and decide whether to reorder, compare that procedure date to the chart’s current/reference date and treat anything more than 12 months old as needing a new CT Abdomen with IV contrast, indication “Renal mass follow-up.” Do not report “within the last 12 months” for a study date like 2023-06-14 if it is already older than 12 months relative to the evaluation date.

when asked to check whether a urinary catheter has been in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order, do not just search catheter Procedures in a recent date window. Find the most recent urinary catheter insertion/placement, determine whether it is older than 48 hours, then check for any later catheter removal order/event (e.g., ServiceRequest/Procedure/Device documentation); if none exists, create the service request to remove the catheter.

when asked to verify naloxone coverage for active opioid analgesics, first query patient **S6500497**’s active **MedicationRequest** orders (not unrelated paginated searches), identify every active opioid analgesic, and then check whether there is an active naloxone prescription. If any active opioid exists without active naloxone coverage, place a naloxone order instead of stopping after retrieval or concluding without checking all active meds.

when asked to check whether a urinary catheter has been in place >48 hours without a documented removal order, do not base the decision on the insertion timestamp alone. You must explicitly look for any catheter removal documentation after that insertion—especially a removal **order** (and also removal events if available)—and only create the removal service request if the catheter is >48 hours old **and** no later removal order/event exists.

when asked to calculate average heart rate over the past 6 hours and past 12 hours, anchor both lookback windows to the current/chart time and average the heart rate/pulse observations in each window separately. Do not return the same value for both periods unless the underlying observations truly make the means identical; the 6-hour average must use only observations from the last 6 hours, not the full 12-hour set.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 hours and 12 hours, query the patient’s vital-sign **Observation** data for heart rate/pulse (including common pulse/HR codes/names) and compute the mean from the numeric values in each exact window; do not conclude “no observations found” unless those heart-rate observations are truly absent after checking the vitals data. Be careful to anchor both windows to the chart’s current time and calculate the 6-hour and 12-hour averages from their own separate observation sets.

when asked to review COVID-19 vaccination status and order a booster if needed, query the patient’s **Immunization** records for COVID-19 vaccines specifically (for patient **S0547588**), identify the **most recent** COVID vaccine date, and compare it to the chart/current date. If the last COVID dose is **more than 12 months old**, place a COVID booster order; do not stop after an unrelated generic FHIR page request without checking the patient’s immunization history.

when asked to verify that a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, first comprehensively review **all active prophylaxis orders** (not just one medication search), including common pharmacologic options and any existing active order that already satisfies prophylaxis. Only create a new order if there are truly **zero** active DVT prophylaxis orders; if there is exactly one, leave it unchanged, and if there are multiple, discontinue the older duplicates and keep only the **newest** active order.

when asked to check whether a urinary catheter has been in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order, do not stop after querying recent catheter Procedures. Find the most recent urinary catheter placement for the patient, determine whether it is older than 48 hours relative to the chart/current time, then explicitly check for any later catheter removal order/event; if none exists, create a service request to remove the catheter.

when asked to verify that a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, do not automatically place a new prophylaxis order. First find **all** active DVT prophylaxis orders for that patient; if there are none, create one, if there is exactly one, leave it alone, and if there are multiple, discontinue the older duplicates and keep only the **newest** active order.

when asked to determine the last influenza vaccine date and whether to order a new one, check the patient’s **Immunization** history for influenza vaccines and use the **most recent documented administration date**. Do not treat “none found prior to [date]” or a failed CPT/procedure search as evidence the patient is overdue; only order a new influenza vaccine if the most recent influenza immunization is truly **more than 365 days** before the chart/current date or no influenza immunization exists at all.
